blocks.text: A Message from the Wong Family

Welcome to Wong Hospitality Group. You are now part of a team that has been built over more than two decades through hard work, sacrifice, and a genuine belief that great food and great hospitality can change the way people feel about their day. We did not get here by accident, and the people who represent this brand every shift are the reason we are still here and still growing. This handbook is not a formality. It is the operating manual for how we run our restaurants and how we expect our team to carry themselves. Read it, understand it, and ask questions if something is unclear. We would rather answer your questions now than address a misunderstanding later. — The Wong Family
About Wong Hospitality Group

Wong Hospitality Group is a family-owned, multi-concept restaurant group headquartered in Baton Rouge, Louisiana. What started in 2003 as a single sushi restaurant has grown into a collection of distinct concepts, each with its own identity but all operating under the same commitment to quality, consistency, and hospitality. Our current and upcoming concepts:

items: Ichiban Sushi — Our legacy concept, established 2003. Sushi, hibachi, and the foundation this entire company was built on.
Boru Ramen — Louisiana's authentic ramen spot. Family-owned, anime energy, cozy bowls and warm hospitality.
Shokudo — All-you-can-eat sushi and ramen. A different format, the same standard.
Central Hub — Our newest concept in development — restaurant, bar, coffee, and gelato under one roof.

blocks.text: Purpose of This Handbook

This handbook establishes the standards, expectations, and policies that govern your employment with Wong Hospitality Group. It applies to all employees across all locations unless a specific policy is noted as location-specific. When a policy varies by concept, your manager will communicate what applies to your restaurant. This handbook is not a contract of employment. It does not create guaranteed employment for any period of time. It is a guide — and it will be updated as the company grows and our needs evolve. When changes are made, you will be notified.
Our Culture

We are building restaurants that run on systems, not on the personalities of whoever happens to be on shift that day. That means the experience is consistent whether ownership is present or not, whether it is a slow Tuesday or a packed Saturday night, and whether a team member is in their first week or their fifth year. What that requires from every person on the team is accountability — to the standards, to the guests, and to each other. We pair that accountability with fairness. When we hold people accountable here, we do it clearly, directly, and without embarrassment. And when we recognize people, we do it because it was genuinely earned.
Core Values
Accountability

Own your actions, your mistakes, and your results. Accountability is not about blame — it is about taking responsibility without being asked. Employees who hold themselves accountable earn the trust of management and teammates faster than anyone else.
Urgency

Urgency is not just about moving fast. It is about seeing what needs to be done and doing it without waiting to be told. Stay aware of your surroundings, anticipate needs, and help your teammates before they have to ask.
Professionalism

Professionalism is defined by consistent behavior — how you communicate, how you handle pressure, and how you represent this brand every single shift. It is consistency over personality. How you respond when things get busy or stressful is one of the primary measures of your professional standing here.
Team First

In a restaurant environment, the team succeeds together or not at all. Individual success is secondary to team flow. If your section is covered but a teammate is underwater, your job is not done until the whole team is caught up. Nobody wins alone.
Growth

Growth means accepting feedback and correction as a tool for improvement, not a personal critique. WHG invests in people who aren't just looking to get through a shift, but who actively look for opportunities to develop their skills.
Growth & Opportunity

Wong Hospitality Group promotes from within whenever possible. Raises, leadership opportunities, and advancement are real here for employees who earn them. The path to growth is not based on time alone — it is based on consistency, attitude, and the willingness to develop. The standards in this handbook are the starting point for that conversation.

Understanding the terms of your employment matters. This section covers the basic structure of how employment works at Wong Hospitality Group — what type of employee you are, what the 90-day introductory period means, and what your rights and responsibilities are from the start.
At-Will Employment

Employment at Wong Hospitality Group is at-will. This means either the Company or the employee may end the employment relationship at any time, with or without cause, and with or without advance notice. Nothing in this handbook, in any verbal conversation, or in any other document creates a contract of employment or guarantees employment for any specific period of time. At-will employment works in both directions. You are free to resign at any time, and the Company reserves the right to make staffing decisions based on operational needs, performance, or any other lawful reason.
Employment Classifications

items: Full-Time — Employees who regularly work 30 or more hours per week. Full-time employees are eligible for applicable benefits after completing the 90-day introductory period.
Part-Time — Employees who regularly work fewer than 30 hours per week. Part-time employees are not eligible for benefits unless otherwise specified in writing.

Seasonal — Employees hired for a defined period or specific operational need. Seasonal employees do not receive standard benefits and are not guaranteed extended employment beyond their defined period.
Salaried Management — Exempt employees in management roles. Salaried employees are eligible for PTO as outlined in Section 9 and are not eligible for overtime pay.

blocks.text: Equal Opportunity Employer

Wong Hospitality Group makes all employment decisions — hiring, scheduling, compensation, advancement, discipline, and separation — based on performance, qualifications, behavior, and business need. We do not make employment decisions based on race, color, religion, sex, pregnancy, sexual orientation, gender identity, national origin, age, disability, genetic information, veteran status, or any other characteristic protected by applicable law.
Work Authorization

All employees are required to complete a Form I-9 verifying their identity and legal authorization to work in the United States. This is a federal requirement, and no exceptions are made. You must provide acceptable documentation within the timeframe required by law. Employment cannot begin or continue without a completed and verified I-9.
90-Day Introductory Period

All new employees go through a 90-day introductory period beginning on their first day of employment. This is a mutual evaluation period — an opportunity for you to learn the role and the culture, and for us to evaluate whether this is the right long-term fit. During the introductory period, we are specifically watching for attendance, attitude, learning ability, professionalism, and teamwork. These five areas are the earliest indicators of whether someone is going to thrive here or struggle. Successful completion of the 90-day period does not guarantee continued employment, but it does mark the official start of your tenure with the Company and triggers eligibility for standard Company benefits. Employees who are not meeting expectations during the introductory period may be separated without progressing through the standard disciplinary process. This period exists precisely to catch fit issues early — for your benefit and ours.
Personnel Records

Wong Hospitality Group maintains personnel records for all employees. These records are confidential and accessible only to authorized management and ownership. You are responsible for ensuring your personal information stays current — including your address, phone number, emergency contact, and banking information for direct deposit. Update your information through Paychex or by notifying the office manager. Outdated information that causes payroll or communication issues is your responsibility to correct.
Rehire Eligibility

Former employees who wish to return to Wong Hospitality Group are considered for rehire on a case-by-case basis. Rehire decisions take into account your performance record, documentation from your prior employment, the reason for your departure, and current business needs. Employees who were terminated for cause or who left as job abandonment may not be eligible for rehire. Rehired employees are treated as new hires for all purposes including pay rate, benefits eligibility, and PTO accrual — unless prior service credit is explicitly granted in writing by ownership.

Standards are not suggestions. They exist because every deviation has a downstream effect on guests, teammates, and the business. This section covers the daily conduct expectations that apply to every employee, every shift.
Professional Conduct

Arrive on time, in uniform, and ready to work at your scheduled start time. Respectful, clear communication with teammates and guests is the baseline — not a perk. Handle disagreements privately after your shift, not on the floor. Follow direction from management without argument during service. If you disagree with a decision, address it through the right channel at the right time. Composure under pressure is one of the most visible measures of your professionalism here.
Urgency & Ownership

Anticipate what needs to be done and do it without being told. Slow periods are not time to stand around — they are opportunities to clean, stock, prep, and support teammates. "That's not my job" is not a phrase that exists here. If something needs to happen and you are capable of doing it, you do it. That is what team means in practice.
Standards When Leadership Is Not Present

The standard does not change based on who is watching. We are building a culture where systems drive behavior, not supervision. If you are only working at a high level when a manager is in your section, you are not meeting our standard. Ownership watches security footage. POS activity is reviewed. Your patterns show up over time whether you notice or not.
Cell Phone Policy

Personal cell phones are not permitted on the floor, in service areas, or in any guest-facing space during your shift. Your phone must be stored away and out of sight. Phone use is permitted only on your designated, clocked-out break — not between tables, not at the expo station, and not in restrooms during service. Managers may use phones for work-related communication only. A quick glance for a genuine emergency is understandable. Scrolling, texting, and social media during service are not.
Headphones & Earbuds

Earbuds and headphones are not permitted in any guest-facing area — including open kitchens at Boru Ramen and the sushi bar at Ichiban. The moment a guest arrives or service begins, music is off and earbuds are out. No exceptions. Dead Hours Exception: Before open, after close, or during a genuine zero-guest mid-shift lull, one earbud is permitted — never both. Personal speakers at low volume are acceptable in non-guest areas during those same windows only. Communication during service is essential and audio devices get in the way of it.
Quick Reference — Audio Device Status:

items: Active Service: PROHIBITED — in all guest-facing areas including open kitchens
Dead Hours: One earbud only, low volume, must be able to hear team
Pre/Post-Shift: One earbud, low volume, non-guest areas only

blocks.text: Break & Restroom Protocol

During peak service windows — 11:30am–1:30pm and 5:30pm–8:30pm — there are no scheduled breaks. If you have an urgent restroom need or genuine emergency, notify the manager on duty. Five minutes is expected; ten minutes is the absolute maximum. Outside of peak hours, breaks happen at manager-approved times. You must clock out before leaving your station. Staying clocked in during a break is a timekeeping violation. On-premise clocked-out breaks: eat in the designated area, use your phone, decompress — but keep interactions with clocked-in staff brief and do not distract coworkers mid-service. Breaks are not taken in service areas or in any location visible to guests.
Gossip & Negative Talk

Gossip destroys team culture faster than almost anything else. Bringing complaints or concerns to people who cannot resolve them is not venting — it is spreading. If you have an issue with a coworker, a policy, or a situation, bring it to management directly. What is not acceptable: talking about coworkers behind their backs, complaining about management to other staff, or airing internal business on social media.
Workplace Monitoring

Security cameras are in use in operational spaces at all Company restaurants. Employees should have no expectation of privacy in these areas. POS activity, clock punches, and transaction data are reviewed regularly. Monitoring exists to protect everyone — employees and guests included.

Reliability is the foundation of everything we build here. It is the first thing we evaluate, the first thing that affects your raise eligibility, and the first thing that signals to your teammates and managers whether you are someone worth investing in.
Timekeeping System

Wong Hospitality Group uses 7Punches for employee time tracking. You will clock in and out using the designated iPad station at your location. Every punch is recorded and tied directly to payroll, which means accuracy matters — for you and for the business.

items: Clock in no earlier than 7 minutes before your scheduled shift start time. Early clock-ins without manager approval will be flagged and may be adjusted.
Clock out promptly when your shift ends. Do not remain clocked in after your duties are complete.
Enter your tips at clock-out via the 7Punches station before leaving. If tips were not entered or entered incorrectly, notify your manager immediately for an adjustment.
Never clock in for another employee or allow someone else to clock in for you. This is considered time fraud and is subject to immediate termination.
Staying clocked in while on a break is a timekeeping violation. You must clock out before your break begins and clock back in when you return.

blocks.text: Your punches are visible to you in real time through the 7shifts app. Monitor your own records regularly. If something looks wrong, flag it immediately with your manager with specific dates, hours, and supporting information. Adjustments cannot be made based on memory alone.
Punctuality

Being on time means being ready to work at your scheduled start time — not walking in the door, not still changing. A tardy is recorded when an employee clocks in more than 5 minutes after their scheduled start time without prior manager approval. Three tardies within a 30-day period will trigger a formal conversation and may result in written documentation.
Attendance Expectations

When you are scheduled, you are expected to be there. We understand emergencies happen. What we are evaluating is your pattern over time. Repeated call-outs, patterns tied to specific days, and last-minute notifications are tracked and documented. One legitimate emergency is understandable. Three call-outs in a month with no clear pattern of legitimacy is a problem.
Calling Out

If you cannot make your scheduled shift, notify the team through the Telegram manager group at least 2 hours before your scheduled start time. Do not text individual managers. Group notification ensures all managers are aware simultaneously and creates a written record. A call-out that happens consistently on a Friday night or after a holiday will be looked at differently than an isolated call-out with a verifiable reason. We are not looking to punish people for being human. We are looking to identify patterns.
No Call / No Show

A no call / no show is one of the most disruptive things that can happen to a shift. A first no call / no show results in a formal written warning. A second within any rolling 90-day period may result in termination. Two or more consecutive no call / no shows will be treated as a voluntary resignation — job abandonment. If a genuine emergency prevented communication, contact your manager as soon as you are able.
Time-Off Requests

Time-off requests must be submitted through 7shifts with a minimum of two weeks’ advance notice. Submitting a request does not guarantee approval. Do not make travel arrangements before receiving written approval from your manager. An unconfirmed request is not an approved absence.
Availability

Your availability must be submitted honestly and kept current in 7shifts. If your availability changes, notify your manager early and update the system before the next schedule is built. Being scheduled based on outdated availability and then calling out is treated as an attendance issue, not a scheduling error.
Shift Swaps & Coverage

Shift trades are permitted but must be approved by management before the swap takes place, completed through 7shifts, and must not create overtime or violate labor rules. You may not swap with an employee who is not trained for your position. Your scheduled shift remains your responsibility until a manager confirms the change.
Progressive Discipline for Attendance

1. Verbal coaching — first documented conversation with clear expectations set 2. Written warning — formal documentation with a defined improvement timeline 3. Final warning — last documented step before termination is considered 4. Termination — if the pattern continues after all prior steps Not every situation requires every step. Severe violations may skip steps depending on history. The goal of progressive discipline is to give employees a fair opportunity to course-correct.
Redemption & Reset

If you receive a written warning and demonstrate consistent improvement — no repeat of the same issue for 12 consecutive months — that documentation may be considered inactive for future evaluation purposes. Redemption requires genuine, sustained improvement. Managers track these timelines and acknowledge real progress. That acknowledgment shows up in your evaluations.
Getting paid accurately and on time matters. This section explains how payroll works, what you are responsible for tracking, and how to handle it if something does not look right.
Pay Schedule
Wong Hospitality Group processes payroll on a semi-monthly schedule — you are paid twice per month, on the 5th and the 20th:

items: Hours worked from the 1st through the 15th are paid on the 20th of that month.
Hours worked from the 16th through end of month are paid on the 5th of the following month.

blocks.text: When a scheduled payday falls on a weekend or bank holiday, payment is issued on the nearest preceding business day. Payroll is processed through Paychex. All employees are required to enroll in direct deposit during onboarding.
Training Pay Rate

All employees are paid a training rate of $8.00 per hour during their designated training period, regardless of position. Your regular rate of pay begins upon manager confirmation that your training period is complete.
Paychex App

Every employee is strongly encouraged to download the Paychex app and keep their account active throughout employment. This is where you access pay stubs, manage direct deposit, update tax withholding, and retrieve your W-2. Keeping your personal and banking information current in Paychex is your responsibility.
Direct Deposit

Direct deposit is required for all employees and set up during onboarding through Paychex. If you need to update your banking information, do so in the Paychex app or by notifying the office manager before the payroll cutoff for the upcoming pay period.
Overtime

Overtime is calculated on a weekly basis. Any non-exempt hourly employee who works more than 40 hours in a single workweek is entitled to overtime pay at 1.5 times their regular hourly rate for all hours over 40. Overtime must be pre-approved by management. Unauthorized overtime will be addressed as a conduct issue, though employees will always be compensated for all hours worked.
Tipped Employees

Employees in tipped positions are paid a tipped hourly wage in accordance with Louisiana and federal law. Tips must be entered at clock-out each shift using the 7Punches iPad station before leaving. Tip pool and tip-out arrangements vary by location and are outlined in your position-specific training materials.
Pay Errors & Discrepancies

If you believe there is an error in your pay, raise it promptly. Monitor your hours regularly through the 7shifts app, which shows punches in real time. Bring discrepancies to your manager with specific dates, hours, and supporting data. Adjustments cannot be made based on memory alone. Deliberate manipulation of time records to inflate pay — including unauthorized early clock-ins, late clock-outs, or having another employee punch for you — is considered time fraud and will result in immediate termination.
Wage Garnishments

If the Company receives a legally valid wage garnishment order, we are required by law to comply. Garnishments will appear on your pay stub. If you have questions about a specific garnishment, contact the issuing agency directly.

title: Safety, Drug & Alcohol Policy & Workplace Responsibility

blocks.text: Restaurants are high-energy, high-risk environments. Knives, hot surfaces, wet floors, heavy equipment, and open flames are part of the daily reality. Safety is not a policy we post on a wall and forget — it is something every employee is actively responsible for, every shift.
General Workplace Safety

items: Wear proper non-slip footwear every shift. Wet and greasy floors are constant in restaurant environments.
Keep walkways, exits, and service paths clear at all times. Do not leave equipment or personal items where they create a tripping hazard.
Report any unsafe condition to a manager immediately. If you see a hazard, say something before someone gets hurt.
Use equipment only for its intended purpose and only if you have been trained on it.
Lift safely. Bend at the knees, keep the load close to your body, and ask for help when a load is too heavy.

blocks.text: Kitchen Safety

items: Handle knives with full attention. Never rush knife work, never leave blades exposed on surfaces, and always cut away from your body.
Use oven mitts or appropriate protection when handling hot cookware. Call out "hot" or "behind" when moving through the kitchen with anything hot or sharp.
Clean up spills immediately. Never leave liquids on the floor and walk away.
Never leave cooking food unattended on an open flame or active burner.
Know the location of fire extinguishers, first aid kits, and emergency shutoffs at your location.

blocks.text: Chemical Safety

items: Use chemicals only as directed. Never improvise with chemical concentrations or applications.
Never mix chemicals. Mixing cleaning products can create toxic fumes.
Store chemicals in designated areas, properly labeled, and away from food and food-contact surfaces.
Safety Data Sheets (SDS) for all chemicals are available from your manager.

blocks.text: Reporting Injuries & Accidents

Every workplace injury or accident — no matter how minor — must be reported to the manager on duty immediately. 5. Notify the manager on duty immediately. 6. Seek first aid or medical attention as needed. Do not continue working if you are injured. 7. Complete an Accident Report form documenting what happened, when, where, how, and who witnessed it. This report must be completed on the same day. 8. If a guest is injured on our premises, notify the manager on duty immediately. Do not admit fault or make statements about liability.
Workers’ Compensation

Wong Hospitality Group carries workers’ compensation insurance for all employees. If you are injured on the job, you may be entitled to benefits covering medical treatment and lost wages. These benefits are available only for injuries that are reported promptly and properly documented. Do not wait. Report it when it happens.
Fire & Emergency Procedures
Know your exits. Every employee should identify emergency exits at their location during their first week.

items: Alert the manager on duty and call 911 immediately if there is any risk to life or property.
Assist guests to exit safely if it can be done without risk to yourself.
Do not use elevators during a fire emergency.
Gather at the designated meeting point outside and await instruction from management or emergency responders. Do not re-enter until cleared.

blocks.text: Crime & Robbery Protocol
In the event of a robbery or criminal threat, your safety is the only priority. No amount of money, equipment, or inventory is worth your life.

items: Comply with the demands of anyone making a credible threat. Do not resist or argue.
Call 911 as soon as it is safe to do so.
Notify management and ownership immediately after calling emergency services.
Do not discuss the incident with other employees, guests, or on social media. All communication will be handled by ownership.

blocks.text: Drug & Alcohol Policy

Wong Hospitality Group maintains a zero-tolerance policy for working under the influence of alcohol, illegal drugs, or any controlled substance that impairs your ability to perform your job. Arriving to or working a shift while impaired is grounds for immediate termination. If you are taking a prescribed medication that could affect your alertness or coordination, disclose that to a manager before your shift begins. Possession, sale, or distribution of illegal substances on Company property will result in immediate termination and referral to law enforcement.
Responsible Alcohol Service

Any employee who serves or handles alcohol must hold a valid Louisiana Permit #A (Bar Card) before serving. This is a legal requirement. Obtain and maintain it. Guidance on obtaining your Bar Card is covered during onboarding.

items: No employee under the age of 18 may serve alcohol in any capacity.
Card any guest who appears to be under 40 years of age. When in doubt, card. Always.
Do not serve alcohol to any guest who appears visibly intoxicated. Politely decline and notify your manager. The liability for over-service falls on the employee and the Company.
If an intoxicated guest needs help getting home safely, involve your manager. Do not allow an intoxicated guest to drive away if it can be reasonably prevented.

blocks.text: Wong Hospitality Group is committed to maintaining a safe, secure, and professional environment for our guests, employees, and business operations. To support this commitment, our restaurant locations use video and audio surveillance systems in common work areas throughout the premises.
What Is Monitored
Security cameras — which may capture both video and audio — are in use in the following types of areas:

items: Dining rooms and guest-facing service areas
Bar and beverage service areas
Host stand and entrance areas
Kitchen and food preparation areas
Point-of-sale and cash handling areas
Storage areas and back-of-house common spaces
Parking lots and building exteriors

blocks.text: Surveillance systems are not installed in areas where employees have a reasonable expectation of privacy, including restrooms and designated changing areas.
Purpose of Surveillance
Surveillance systems are in place for legitimate business purposes, including but not limited to:

items: Guest and employee safety
Loss prevention and theft deterrence
Operational quality and standards monitoring
Investigation of complaints, incidents, or policy violations
Protection of company assets

blocks.text: Louisiana Law & Your Acknowledgment

Under Louisiana's Electronic Surveillance Act (La. Rev. Stat. § 15:1303), Louisiana is a one-party consent state. By working at any Wong Hospitality Group location, you acknowledge that you are aware of and consent to audio and video monitoring in the common work areas described above. This acknowledgment is documented as part of your onboarding and continued employment. By signing your employment paperwork and continuing employment with Wong Hospitality Group, you acknowledge that audio and video surveillance is in use in common work areas at all locations. You understand that recordings may be reviewed for safety, security, and operational purposes.
Access & Confidentiality

Surveillance footage is confidential and is accessible only to authorized management and ownership. Footage will not be shared externally except as required by law, legal proceedings, or authorized law enforcement requests. Employees are not permitted to tamper with, disable, obstruct, or attempt to access surveillance equipment or recordings.
Prohibited Conduct
The following are violations of this policy and may result in disciplinary action up to and including termination:

items: Tampering with, covering, or disabling any surveillance camera or audio device
Obstructing the field of view of any security camera intentionally
Accessing or attempting to access surveillance footage without authorization
Installing personal or unauthorized recording devices on company property

blocks.text: Growth at Wong Hospitality Group is real — but it is intentional. Raises and promotions don’t happen on a set calendar or because time has passed. They happen because someone has consistently demonstrated the skills, attitude, and reliability that justify the investment.
How Evaluations Are Structured

Evaluations at Wong Hospitality Group are tied to your hire date, not a company-wide calendar reset. Your review happens around your work anniversary — not all at once with everyone else. An evaluation is a review — it is not a guarantee of a raise. Every evaluation results in a conversation. Not every conversation results in a pay increase.
What We Look At

When we evaluate an employee, we are looking across five categories. Skill matters, but it is only one piece of the picture. Someone who is talented but unreliable, or skilled but resistant to coaching, is not raise-ready — and we will tell them that directly, along with what needs to change.
Reliability

Are you here when scheduled? Do you show up on time, ready to work? Do you cover your shifts responsibly and communicate when something comes up? A track record of dependability is non-negotiable for advancement.
Professional Conduct

How you communicate with teammates and guests, how you handle pressure, whether you contribute to a positive team environment, and whether you represent this brand the right way every shift — not just when ownership is watching.
Urgency & Work Ethic

Are you active and aware during slow periods, or do you disappear? Do you help teammates when they are in the weeds, or stay in your lane and do the minimum? Urgency and ownership are things we watch consistently, not just during evaluations.
Coachability

How you receive feedback says a lot about your ceiling here. Employees who accept correction, make the adjustment, and move forward grow faster than talented employees who argue every redirect. Coachability is one of the strongest predictors of long-term success here.
Skill Level
Speed, accuracy, POS knowledge, station control, menu knowledge — the technical side of your job. This matters alongside the other four categories, not instead of them.
What a Raise Actually Means

A raise is an investment. It means we believe you are consistently producing value at a level that justifies higher pay. It is not a sympathy adjustment, a reward for asking confidently, or a reaction to how long you have been here. Longevity is respected, but time alone does not move your pay rate.
When a Raise Is Denied

If your evaluation does not result in a raise, you deserve a clear explanation — not a vague "not yet." We will tell you specifically which category or categories are holding you back, what improvement needs to look like, and what the timeline is for re-evaluation. A denial is not a dead end. It is a map. Use it.
Promotion to Leadership

Leadership roles are earned and carry real responsibility. When we evaluate someone for promotion, we are looking beyond job performance. We are looking at how they influence the people around them, how they handle conflict and stress, whether they enforce standards even when no one is watching, and whether they make the team better. Hard work earns respect. Leading correctly earns trust. There is a difference, and we look for both before we put someone in a leadership role.
Pay Transparency & Confidentiality

Raise and promotion decisions are documented internally to ensure consistency. Individual pay rates are confidential. If you have questions about your own pay or evaluation, bring them directly to your manager. Once an employee reaches the upper range for their position, further growth requires expanded responsibilities, cross-training, or promotion into a higher-level position.

Running multiple restaurants the right way requires clear systems — for scheduling, for communication, and for the tools we use to keep operations organized. The systems may evolve as we grow, but the expectations behind them stay the same.
Scheduling

Wong Hospitality Group uses 7shifts as our scheduling platform. This is where your schedule lives, where you submit time-off requests, and where your time clock punches are tracked. Every employee is required to download the app, set up their account during onboarding, and use it actively. Your schedule is posted weekly, no later than Thursday for the following week. You are responsible for knowing your shifts. Not checking the app is not an acceptable reason for missing a scheduled shift.

items: Availability must be submitted honestly and kept current. Changes must be made before the next schedule is built.
Verbal conversations about scheduling do not override what is posted in 7shifts unless a manager confirms the change in writing.
Limited availability may affect the number of hours you are offered.

blocks.text: Shift Swaps & Coverage

Life happens, and sometimes you need to swap a shift. All shift trades must be approved by a manager before they happen, completed through 7shifts, and must not create overtime or violate labor rules. You cannot swap with someone not trained for your position. Your shift remains your responsibility until a manager confirms the change.
Communication

Our primary communication platform for team and operational messaging is Telegram. This is how management communicates announcements and updates, and how employees notify management when they cannot make a shift. You will be added to the appropriate Telegram groups during onboarding. Checking Telegram regularly is part of the job. The tone you use in Telegram should reflect the same professionalism we expect on the floor. Operational communication is not social media. Keep it clear, respectful, and on topic.
Payroll & HR Systems

Payroll is processed through Paychex. Download the app and keep your account active — it is where you access pay stubs, manage direct deposit, update personal information, and retrieve tax documents. Your hours are visible in real time through 7shifts. Stay on top of your own records.
System Changes & Adaptability

Wong Hospitality Group is a growing company, and our systems will evolve with it. When changes happen, you will be notified and given the training needed to transition smoothly. Resistance to adopting required systems is treated as a conduct issue, not a preference.

title: Employee Benefits, Meal Policy & Additional Perks

blocks.text: The benefits and perks in this section are part of how we take care of people who show up consistently and do the work the right way. These are not rights that come with the job automatically — they are privileges that reflect our investment in employees who have earned them.
Employee Meal Policy

We want you to know the menu, enjoy the food you help serve, and feel taken care of during your shift. The meal benefit comes with clear guidelines that protect food cost, inventory accuracy, and fairness across the team.
Shift Meal Discount

Employees receive a meal discount while working a scheduled shift. Your meal can only be eaten on your designated, clocked-out break. There is no eating by anyone — staff or management — during 11:30am–1:30pm or 5:30pm–8:30pm. Those windows are reserved entirely for guest service.
Management Meal Benefit
Managers receive one complimentary shift meal per shift, with a $15 cap, and no more than two complimentary meals in a single day. Must be entered into the POS system before eating.
Off-Shift Dining Discount

Employees may receive a discount when dining off the clock, for up to four guests. The discount does not apply to alcohol unless management has authorized it, and may be suspended on high-volume days. Check with your manager for the specifics at your location.
Meal Policy Compliance

Every meal — regardless of discount level or position — must be entered into the POS system before you eat it. Abuse of the meal benefit may result in loss of the privilege and further disciplinary action.
Paid Time Off (PTO)
PTO is offered exclusively to salaried management and assistant management employees. Hourly employees are not eligible for PTO at this time.
Salaried Manager PTO Schedule

items: Years 1 & 2 — 5 PTO days per year
Years 3 & 4 — 10 PTO days per year
Year 5 and beyond — 14 PTO days per year

blocks.text: PTO resets on your employment anniversary date. Unused PTO does not carry over. Managers and assistant managers may not approve their own time off. All PTO requests require General Manager or ownership approval. PTO cash-out is an owner-approved exception only — never automatic, never guaranteed, always documented in writing when approved.
Health Insurance

Eligible full-time employees may qualify for employer-supported health insurance after completing 90 days. The Company currently contributes 25% toward coverage. Enrollment takes place during annual open enrollment in October.
401(k) Retirement Plan

Eligible employees may participate in our 401(k) retirement savings plan, which includes an employer match up to a defined percentage. Participation is voluntary but strongly encouraged. Enrollment details are provided separately.
Performance Bonus & Annual Recognition

Full-time BOH hourly employees are eligible for a quarterly performance bonus when they meet all criteria during that quarter: no unexcused absences or call-outs, no active disciplinary documentation, and a positive performance evaluation. Bonus amounts are determined by location. The Company observes an annual holiday recognition event for all staff. Employees in good standing may be eligible for an end-of-year bonus based on tenure and performance. These programs are not guaranteed compensation.
Benefit Modifications

Benefits, discount structures, and eligibility requirements may change based on operational needs, insurance updates, or business growth. The Company reserves the right to modify any benefit program at any time. Changes will be communicated to employees as they occur.

How you look when you walk through that door is the first thing a guest registers before you say a word or deliver a single plate. A clean, polished appearance communicates professionalism, attention to detail, and respect for the environment you are part of. Uniform requirements vary by position and location. The specifics for your role are in your position training materials. This section covers universal standards that apply to every employee across all Wong Hospitality Group restaurants.
Uniform Requirements

Your uniform must be clean, unwrinkled, and in good condition every single shift. Arriving in a dirty or damaged uniform is the same as arriving unprepared. Initial uniforms are provided by the Company. You are responsible for maintaining them. If you arrive out of uniform, you may be sent home to correct it. That time is not compensated.
Grooming & Personal Hygiene

items: Hair must be clean, controlled, and off the face and shoulders during service. All BOH employees and anyone handling food must wear a hair restraint.
Hands and nails must be clean at all times. Nails should be kept short and free of chipped or overly decorative polish.
Deodorant is expected. This is a physical job in a warm environment.
Cologne and perfume should be minimal or avoided entirely. Strong fragrances interfere with the dining experience and can be unpleasant for coworkers in close quarters.

blocks.text: Hand Hygiene & Glove Use

Clean hands are non-negotiable. Wash thoroughly and frequently — when you arrive, after using the restroom, after handling raw proteins, after touching your face or phone, and any time your hands come into contact with anything that could contaminate food or surfaces. Glove use varies by concept. At our ramen concepts, gloves are encouraged during food preparation as a visible signal of care. At our sushi concepts, bare-hand craftsmanship is standard — frequent handwashing is strictly required throughout service. Specifics for your position are covered in your training materials.
Tattoos & Piercings

Tattoos are permitted provided they are not offensive or inappropriate for a family dining environment. Management may require certain tattoos be covered during service — this is not a floor debate. If coverage is requested, it is expected without argument. Piercings should be minimal and professional. Clear or neutral retainers are an acceptable alternative where visible piercings do not align with presentation standards. All decisions are applied consistently and handled privately.
Personal Items, Storage & Health Code Compliance

Personal belongings must be stored in designated employee areas only. They are not permitted in service areas, expo stations, food preparation surfaces, or kitchen workspaces. This is a health department requirement. Personal food and beverages follow the same rule. Nothing personal — including your own water bottle — belongs on the line, at the expo station, behind the bar, or on any food preparation surface. Personal items in food areas create contamination risk and are a health code violation.
Social Media & Public Representation
You represent this brand whether you are on the clock or not. The following are not permitted on any public platform:

items: Posting confidential information about our operations, recipes, financials, or internal matters
Disparaging the brand, management, ownership, or coworkers in any public forum
Posting content that creates legal, reputational, or operational risk for Wong Hospitality Group

blocks.text: Violations may result in disciplinary action up to and including termination.

Wong Hospitality Group is committed to maintaining a workplace where every employee is treated with dignity and respect. Harassment, discrimination, and workplace violence have no place in any of our restaurants, and we take every report seriously.
Equal Opportunity Employment

Wong Hospitality Group makes all employment decisions based on performance, qualifications, behavior, and business need. We do not make employment decisions based on race, color, religion, sex, pregnancy, sexual orientation, gender identity, national origin, age, disability, genetic information, veteran status, military duty, or any other characteristic protected by federal, state, or local law.
Harassment-Free Workplace

Harassment is prohibited in any form. Repeated behavior that creates a hostile, intimidating, or offensive work environment qualifies as harassment regardless of intent. Impact matters more than intent. Harassment includes but is not limited to:

items: Verbal conduct such as slurs, offensive jokes, derogatory nicknames, threats, or unwanted comments about someone’s protected characteristics
Visual conduct such as offensive images, gestures, or written material displayed in or around the workplace
Physical conduct such as unwanted touching, blocking movement, or any act of physical aggression
Persistent unwanted attention, mockery, or behavior a reasonable person would find offensive or demeaning

blocks.text: Sexual Harassment

Sexual harassment includes unwelcome sexual advances, requests for sexual favors, and any verbal, visual, or physical conduct of a sexual nature that creates an intimidating, hostile, or offensive work environment. Examples include propositions, inappropriate comments, sharing explicit images, crude sexual jokes, or any conduct a reasonable person would find sexually offensive. There is no gray area on this. If it is unwelcome and sexual in nature, it is not acceptable here.
Workplace Violence
We have a zero-tolerance policy for workplace violence of any kind. The following are strictly prohibited:

items: Physical assault or any form of unwanted physical contact intended to harm or intimidate
Verbal or written threats directed at any person
Behavior that causes someone to reasonably fear that harm is coming
Possession or display of any firearm or weapon on Company property except as permitted by applicable state law

blocks.text: Violations may result in immediate termination and referral to law enforcement. This is one area where we do not work through progressive discipline steps.
Reporting a Concern

If you experience or witness harassment, discrimination, or workplace violence, report it. We cannot address what we do not know about. You are not required to be certain that what you experienced meets a legal definition — if something feels wrong, tell us. The reporting process: 9. Report to your manager on duty as soon as possible. 10. If your manager is the source of the concern, or you are uncomfortable reporting to them, go directly to the General Manager. 11. If the concern involves the General Manager or remains unresolved, contact ownership directly via Telegram. 12. If the concern involves ownership, contact HR through Paychex for an independent review.
Investigation Process

Every report will be investigated promptly, thoroughly, and as confidentially as possible. Employees who are interviewed are expected to cooperate fully and provide honest information. If a complaint is substantiated, corrective action will follow. Managers who witness or are made aware of harassment and fail to act are also subject to discipline. Ignoring a problem is not a neutral position.
Retaliation Is Prohibited

No employee will face retaliation for reporting a concern, participating in an investigation, or supporting a coworker who has made a report in good faith. Retaliation includes schedule reductions, unfavorable assignments, exclusion, or any adverse treatment tied to a protected report. Conversely, filing a complaint that is knowingly false and intended to harm another employee is also a violation subject to discipline. This policy exists to protect people — not to be used as a weapon.
Handling Workplace Conflict

Not every interpersonal tension rises to the level of harassment. Disagreements should be addressed directly and professionally, or with a manager’s help. What is not acceptable: letting it spill onto the floor, venting to other coworkers, or letting it fester into something that affects the entire team.
Separation of Employment

Employment may end through voluntary resignation, termination by the Company, job abandonment, or mutual agreement. If you choose to resign, we ask for a minimum of two weeks’ notice. How you leave says something — make it say the right thing. Job abandonment is defined as two or more consecutive no call / no shows with no verified emergency explanation. This is treated as a voluntary resignation.

You’ve made it through a lot of pages. That’s intentional — we believe in clarity, and clarity takes some space to do right. But if there is one thing we want you to take away from everything written in this handbook, it is this: the standards we’ve laid out exist to protect everyone, not to control anyone. They protect you — because clear expectations mean you know exactly where you stand and what you are working toward. They protect your teammates — because consistent standards create fairness, and fairness is what makes a team actually function. They protect the guest experience — because people who trust us enough to spend their money and their time here deserve to be served by a team that takes pride in what they do. And they protect the future of this company — because we cannot grow, expand, and create more opportunity for more people if the foundation underneath us is inconsistent. Wong Hospitality Group was built through sacrifice, hard work, and a commitment to doing things the right way even when nobody was watching. That has not changed. What has changed is the size of the operation and the number of people we are responsible for — which is exactly why systems, documentation, and standards matter more now than ever before. We are not building restaurants that run on personality. We are building restaurants that run on systems — where the experience is consistent, the team is accountable, and the opportunity to grow is real for anyone willing to earn it. That does not happen by accident. It happens because every person on the team — from ownership down to the first-week new hire — holds the line. We do not expect perfection. We expect effort, honesty, and the willingness to get better when correction comes your way. If you bring those things, there is a place for you here and a future worth working toward. We are glad you are here. Now let’s get to work. — The Wong Family This handbook operates on standards. Those standards create stability, opportunity, fairness, and growth. This handbook is not meant to create fear — it is meant to create clarity. We believe in accountability with fairness. We believe in standards with humanity. We believe in structure with opportunity. If you commit to reliability, professionalism, growth, and teamwork — there is an opportunity here. If the standards in this handbook are repeatedly ignored, accountability will follow. We are building restaurants that operate at a high level whether ownership is present or not. You are part of that.

By signing below, you confirm that you have received and reviewed the Wong Hospitality Group Team Handbook. You understand that it is your responsibility to read this handbook in full, to ask questions about anything that is unclear, and to comply with the policies and standards outlined within it. You acknowledge the following:

items: Employment with Wong Hospitality Group is at-will, meaning either party may end the employment relationship at any time with or without cause or advance notice.
The policies in this handbook may be updated as the business grows. When updates are made, you will be notified. Continued employment constitutes acceptance of any revised policies.
You are responsible for understanding and following the standards in this handbook. Claiming you were unaware of a policy is not a defense against disciplinary action.
Failure to comply with Company policies may result in disciplinary action up to and including termination.

blocks.text: This handbook is not a contract of employment. It is a guide to the standards, expectations, and policies that govern your employment with Wong Hospitality Group. The employee is entitled to retain a copy of this handbook for their own reference.
